---
title: "Delegated Coding"
subtitle: "Managing your AI collaborators"
author: "Anna Woodard"
institute: "UChicago Data Science Institute · UChicago Medicine"
date: 2026-06-18
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    width: 1280          # 16:9 widescreen (PowerPoint-shaped)
    height: 720
    slide-number: c/t       # current / total, e.g. 3/8
    incremental: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    include-after-body: _logos.html   # both logos on every slide
execute:
  echo: false      # don't show code unless a slide asks for it
  freeze: auto     # cache results so a rebuild doesn't recompute everything
---

<!--
CHEAT SHEET (delete before presenting)

Build:        quarto render slides.qmd          -> slides.html
Live preview: quarto preview slides.qmd         -> auto-reloads in browser

New slide:        start a line with "## Title"
Section divider:  "# SECTION TITLE"
Speaker notes:    ::: {.notes}  ... :::   (press 's' in browser for presenter view)
Two columns:      ::: {.columns} / ::: {.column width="50%"} ... ::: / :::
Incremental list: add {.incremental} after a "##" heading
Fragment reveal:  add [text]{.fragment}

TIMING PLAN (60 min total)
  ~5   hook + reframe
  ~3   what changed
  ~10  mental model (PI / delegate-vs-own)
  ~15  running the lab (workflow)
  ~10  when it goes wrong
  ~7   the student catch
  ~5   getting started + takeaways
  ~5   buffer / questions
-->

## Full disclosure {.center}

This talk was prepared the way it recommends:

I sketched the argument. My agent drafted the slides.

**I own every claim on them.**

::: {.notes}
~1 min. Open with this — it's the whole talk in miniature. The agent did the
typing; I did the thinking and the reviewing. If anything on these slides is
wrong, that's on me, not the tool. That division of labor is the next hour.
:::

## Why not call it "vibe coding"?

*Vibe coding*: prompt, accept, run, hope.

- Fine for a throwaway script or a weekend toy
- Not fine when someone — you, your mentor, a reviewer — has to **trust the result**

. . .

*Delegated coding*: the agent does the work; **you stay in charge and accountable**.

. . .

The skill you'll actually need is not prompting.
It's **management**: scoping tasks, setting standards, reviewing evidence.

::: {.notes}
~4 min. The REU students have all vibe-coded already — don't pretend they
haven't. The pitch: research raises the stakes. Vibes don't survive a
methods section. "Managing your AI collaborators" is meant literally: by
the end of the summer you'll supervise more code than you write.
:::

## What changed: the unit of delegation grew

| Era | Tool | You delegate... |
|---|---|---|
| 2021 | autocomplete (Copilot) | a **line** |
| 2023 | chat (ChatGPT) | a **function** you paste back |
| now | agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor) | a **task** |

. . .

An agent reads your repo, edits files, **runs commands**, sees the errors,
and iterates — for minutes to hours, without you watching.

::: {.notes}
~3 min. Key distinction for anyone who's only used the chat workflow: agents
close the loop themselves. They run the code, read the traceback, fix it,
rerun. That's why a "task" is now the unit — and why management, not
prompting, is the skill. Live show of Claude Code here if time allows.
:::

# You are the PI {background-color="#800000"}

## The mental model

The agent is a fast, tireless, overconfident **junior collaborator**.

A PI doesn't watch every line get typed. A PI owns:

- the **question**
- what **evidence** would answer it
- what would **change our minds**
- the **constraints** — data provenance, inclusion criteria, definitions of success

. . .

Agents map onto this surprisingly well. So does its failure mode:
a student who never gets supervised produces work you can't trust.

::: {.notes}
~4 min. This is the load-bearing slide. Everything later — AGENTS.md as a
lab manual, meeting-sized tasks, auditing — falls out of taking this
analogy seriously. Also flag the asymmetry honestly: unlike a PhD student,
the agent doesn't learn between sessions unless YOU write things down.
That's foreshadowing for AGENTS.md.
:::

## The prompt shift

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="42%"}
**Micromanaging**

> "Fix this line."

> "Now change the axis label."

> "No, the other axis."
:::
::: {.column width="58%"}
**Delegating**

> "Here is the **goal**, here is the repo, here is **what would convince me**.
> Inspect first, propose a **plan**, implement the **smallest thing** that
> answers the question, run a **reproducible check**, and tell me
> **what changed**."
:::
:::

. . .

You don't have to watch every line —
but you **do** have to review the scientific logic and the outputs.

::: {.notes}
~3 min. This was the single biggest shift in my own usage. The delegating
prompt is doing five jobs: goal, context, acceptance criteria, process
(inspect → plan → implement → verify), and a reporting requirement. Students
can copy this template verbatim.
:::

## What to delegate, what to own

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Delegate** — implementation & mechanical investigation

- "make this faster"
- "write a parser for this format"
- "turn this notebook into a script"
- "find why this Slurm job failed"
- "summarize missingness by cohort"
- "compare these two result folders"
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Own** — question, design, interpretation

- "is this result *real*?"
- "is this comparison *fair*?"
- "does this prove the *mechanism*?"
- "what should we try *next*?"

For these, use the agent as a
**skeptical assistant**, not the
decision-maker.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
~3 min. Rough rule worth saying out loud: if the task has a checkable "done",
delegate it. If the task IS the judgment, keep it. You can still ask the
agent to argue both sides of an interpretation — that's using it as a
skeptic, which is different from letting it decide.
:::

# Running the lab {background-color="#800000"}

## 1. Put the project in git

Non-negotiable, and not just for the usual reasons:

- the agent will **edit your files and run commands** — git is the undo button
- cheap checkpoints: commit before every delegated task
- the diff **is** your review surface: `git diff` shows exactly what your collaborator did

. . .

Bonus: agents are very good at writing commit messages — make them.

::: {.notes}
~2 min. For students who treat git as a chore: reframe it as the supervision
tool. You review an agent the way you review a pull request. No git, no diff,
no review, no trust.
:::

## 2. Write the onboarding doc: `AGENTS.md`

Everything you'd tell a new lab member on day one, in the repo root:

- how to set up the **environment**
- **where the data lives** (and what must never be touched)
- what counts as a **valid result**
- output and naming **conventions**

. . .

The agent reads it automatically at the start of every session.

**Write it once → stop re-explaining your project every morning.**

::: {.notes}
~3 min. Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md conventions automatically;
Cursor and Codex have equivalents. Start small — ten lines is fine — it
grows over the summer. The growth mechanism is the audit-rule flywheel,
coming up in the failure-modes section.
:::

## 3. Give meeting-sized tasks

One task ≈ what you'd bring to one meeting with your mentor:

- "make this plot reproducible from this CSV"
- "turn this notebook cell into a script with arguments"
- "find why this job failed and summarize the fix"

. . .

Not: *"build my whole analysis pipeline"*

- too big to review ⇒ you **can't** stay accountable
- start where you already know what **"done" looks like**

::: {.notes}
~2 min. Task size is the main control knob you have. Too small = you're
micromanaging and gaining nothing. Too big = the review burden exceeds your
ability to verify, and you've silently handed over ownership. Meeting-sized
is the sweet spot: big enough to delegate, small enough to audit.
:::

## 4. Demand the full loop

Ask for **inspect → plan → implement → verify**, not just code.

A good session ends with a report:

1. the **commands** that were run
2. the **artifacts** produced
3. **what changed**
4. the **remaining uncertainty**

. . .

If the agent can't tell you these four things, the task isn't done.

::: {.notes}
~3 min. "Remaining uncertainty" is the item students will skip and the one
that matters most. An agent that says "done, all tests pass" is less useful
than one that says "done, but I assumed the date column is UTC — check
that." You can literally put this four-item report format in AGENTS.md.
:::

## 5. Make every result auditable

One experiment → **one directory** → one `README` recording provenance:

> command · config · inputs · git state · environment · parameters · **how to regenerate**

. . .

Plus a **lab notebook** the agent maintains:
what was tried, what failed, what we currently believe.

. . .

Encode all of it in `AGENTS.md` — so it happens **every time**, not just when you remember.

::: {.notes}
~3 min. Sell the selfish benefit: resuming after a week away. "What is this
folder? Which run made this figure?" disappears as a category of problem.
And the agent does the bookkeeping — this convention costs you nothing once
it's written down.
:::

## 6. Maintain the workflow itself

Agent work accumulates **slop**: duplicated scripts, dead code, stale instructions.

Every few sessions, delegate a hygiene pass:

> "Read `AGENTS.md` and the recent lab-notebook entries. Identify instructions
> that are outdated, ambiguous, duplicated, or missing based on recent
> failures. Propose a shorter, cleaner version before editing."

. . .

Rule for hygiene passes: **no change to scientific behavior** —
only make the next experiment easier to audit and rerun.

::: {.notes}
~2 min. Treat the workflow as something you maintain, not something you set
up once. This is also a nice example of delegating meta-work: the agent
cleaning up after the agent.
:::

# When it goes wrong {background-color="#800000"}

## It will be wrong in ways that *look* right

The dangerous failures don't throw errors:

- cohort definitions or **denominators silently change** between runs
- it grabs the **wrong version of a file** when several exist
- runs look independent but **share an assumption** — fake stability
- summaries sound **more confident than the evidence supports**

. . .

**Reviewing code is not enough.**
If reading code caught everything, there would never be bugs in code.

::: {.notes}
~3 min. This is the section that earns the talk its keep. The code reads
fine, the plot renders, the numbers are plausible — and the result is wrong.
Every one of these four is a failure I have actually seen. Slow down here.
:::

## Case study: the seed that didn't vary

I asked for an experiment to be rerun **three times** to check stability.

The agent reran it three times — **with the same random seed**.

Three matching results. Zero independent evidence. *Looked great.*

. . .

The fix wasn't "be more careful." It was a new rule in `AGENTS.md`:

> "Before running comparisons or repeats, define **what is supposed to vary**
> and **what is supposed to stay fixed**. After running, **audit the run
> metadata** to verify the intended variables changed and the controlled
> ones didn't."

::: {.notes}
~3 min. Tell this as a story, not a bullet list. Point out the shape of the
fix: not a one-off correction, but the GENERAL version of the mistake,
written down where every future session sees it.
:::

## The flywheel: mistakes → audit rules

You will not list every possible failure in advance. Don't try.

Instead:

1. **Catch** a mistake (you will)
2. Write down the **general version** as a rule in `AGENTS.md`
3. Every future session inherits it

. . .

Your `AGENTS.md` becomes the lab's institutional memory —
the thing a real research group takes **years** to build.

::: {.notes}
~2 min. This reframes errors as compounding assets. It's also the answer to
"the agent doesn't learn between sessions": correct — so the learning has to
live in the repo, and this is the mechanism.
:::

## Make auditing cheap

The catch with working at a higher level: it's easy to miss details.

The answer is **not** micromanagement — it's a small set of artifacts you always inspect:

- the final **figure or table**
- the **command** that made it
- the **inputs actually loaded** and the **cohort counts**
- config **differences between runs** · seeds / splits / folds
- a short note: **what changed, what's uncertain**

. . .

Five minutes of auditing per task. That's the whole price of staying in charge.

::: {.notes}
~2 min. The checklist is deliberately short — if auditing is expensive,
students will skip it. Counts are the highest-value check per second spent:
if N changed and you don't know why, stop.
:::

# The student catch {background-color="#800000"}

## Don't delegate the learning

The PI model has a prerequisite: **the PI knows the field.**

You're here this summer to *become* someone who can supervise —
and you can only delegate what you could **evaluate yourself**.

. . .

- It makes a competent person **faster**
- It makes a confused person **more confidently wrong**

. . .

If you couldn't judge whether the output is right, that task wasn't yours to delegate yet.

::: {.notes}
~3 min. The honest tension in this talk: I'm teaching delegation to people
whose actual job is learning. Resolution: the delegation boundary moves as
you grow. Week 1 it's small; by August it's most of the implementation. The
skill of KNOWING where your boundary is — that's the real takeaway.
:::

## Make it a tutor, not a vending machine

Don't only ask for code. Ask:

- *"Why this approach over the alternatives?"*
- *"What could go wrong here?"*
- *"What would break if my data looked different?"*
- *"Explain this diff like I'm reviewing it."*

. . .

You have unlimited access to a patient explainer with no ego.
**For a student, the explanation is worth more than the code.**

::: {.notes}
~2 min. This is how delegation and learning coexist: every delegated task is
also a tutoring opportunity if you ask the follow-ups. Reading the agent's
diffs critically teaches code review faster than writing code alone ever did.
:::

## Verify on something you can check

Before trusting it on the case you can't check,
run it on a tiny case where you **already know the answer**.

. . .

Right on the known case → it has *earned some* trust on the unknown one.

. . .

One more thing, because we're at a medical campus:
your **prompt and code** go to a company's servers — your **data doesn't have to**.
Never point a cloud tool at patient data or anything covered by a DUA. Ask first.

::: {.notes}
~2 min. The known-answer check is the single most transferable habit in the
talk — it's just science. The data note: one slide, said plainly, not a
lecture. "Help me write the code" is fine; "here's the dataset, analyze it"
is the thing you never do with sensitive data. When unsure, ask your mentor.
:::

# Getting started {background-color="#800000"}

## Your first delegated task — this week

Pick a **small, annoying, real** task in your own project:

1. `git init` (or commit), write a 10-line `AGENTS.md`
2. Delegate the task end-to-end: goal, context, what would convince you
3. Require the report: commands · artifacts · what changed · uncertainty
4. Audit it: inputs, counts, the diff
5. First mistake you catch → first rule in your `AGENTS.md`

. . .

Don't start with the open-ended project.
**Start where you already know what "done" looks like.**

::: {.notes}
~2 min. Skip the hunt for the perfect tutorial — skim one tool guide and
learn by doing. Tool guides: code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices,
platform.openai.com/docs/codex, docs.cursor.com/agent/modes. (Say these are
linked on the resources slide / will be shared with the deck.)
:::

## Resources

- **Claude Code** — [code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices)
- **Codex** — [platform.openai.com/docs/codex](https://platform.openai.com/docs/codex)
- **Cursor** — [docs.cursor.com/agent/modes](https://docs.cursor.com/agent/modes)

. . .

Don't hunt for the one perfect tutorial.
Skim a guide for whichever tool you have access to, then **learn on a real task**.

## If you remember three things

1. **You are the PI.** Delegate implementation; own the question, the design, and the interpretation.
2. **Write it down once.** `AGENTS.md`, provenance, lab notebook — and turn every caught mistake into an audit rule.
3. **Trust is earned by verification.** Check the counts, the inputs, the diffs — and verify on a case you already know.

. . .

Questions?

::: {.notes}
Leave ~5 min. If one question to seed: "what's the smallest task in your
project you could delegate tomorrow — and how would you know if it came back
wrong?"
:::
